original_abstract: Minority populations remain vastly underrepresented in clinical research, a disparity that has become increasingly critical as cancer burdens and chronic disease rates continue to climb. In the United States, recruitment efforts are frequently stymied by complex barriers, most notably a pervasive lack of trust in institutional medicine. Successful engagement requires a nuanced strategy that respects community history, cultural beliefs, and socioeconomic pressures. Over several decades of global research, we developed a methodology centered on recruiting and training local residents to serve as primary research staff and frontline communicators. Upon establishing our program in the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001, we applied this community-based model to the study of chronic disease. In 2004, we trained a dedicated team of local women who successfully enrolled a longitudinal cohort of over 5,000 Mexican Americans from randomly selected households. This team has since evolved into a highly skilled workforce capable of performing advanced diagnostics, including ultrasonography, FibroScan, retinal imaging, and cognitive assessments. Today, this infrastructure supports several major initiatives, including NIH-funded clinical trials and multi-omic studies of liver disease, obesity, and diabetes. By leveraging local expertise, we are advancing precision medicine approaches to improve the prevention and treatment of chronic diseases in underserved populations.
humanized_abstract: It is unfortunate that minority populations are vastly underrepresented in studies relating to cancer and chronic diseases. This issue has turned into a global crisis due to the rise in cancer and chronic disease rates. In the United States the reasons for the lack of participants in trials and studies vary but are often due to many different barriers which often include lack of trust in the medical institution. In order to engage the participation of minority populations a strategy that is culturally and socially relevant is necessary and it is essential to understand the historical, cultural, social, and economic issues in each particular community. Through years of research in many countries, we developed the model of using community residents as the core of the research staff and as the initial point of contact for participants. Using this approach, the lab moved to the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001 in order to study chronic diseases. In 2004, a group of local women were trained to carry out a longitudinal study on 5,000 Mexican Americans recruited from random selected households. The success of this initial group of participants and staff led to further training and skills that were needed to carry out complex studies such as the carrying out of ultrasonography, FibroScan, retinal scans for eye diseases and cognitive assessments for dementia studies. Presently, this local workforce is involved in several ongoing trials funded by the NIH for a study on the prevention of chronic diseases through clinical trials and multi-omic studies of liver diseases in individuals with obesity and diabetes. Through the use of local personnel, precision medicine approaches are being further explored to the studies of prevention and treatment of chronic diseases in minority populations.
